original_hypothesis.title: FRouter.graduate can be abused by any EXECUTOR_ROLE to drain all assetToken from a pair regardless of token state
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: graduate(tokenAddress) transfers the entire assetToken balance from the pair to msg.sender (the executor), with no checks that graduation conditions are met (e.g., token reached threshold, trading disabled, one-time graduation). Any address holding EXECUTOR_ROLE can pull liquidity at any time, potentially breaking the bonding market and diverting assets.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While role-gated, EXECUTOR_ROLE is often assigned to operational hot wallets/contracts (e.g., Bonding). If compromised or mis-assigned, this becomes an immediate drain. Even without compromise, this is a sharp edge: no invariant enforcement in code.
deployment_assumptions: EXECUTOR_ROLE is assigned to at least one externally reachable contract or operational EOA beyond strictly trusted automation
Pair holds non-trivial assetToken balance (e.g., from trading fees or liquidity)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No scripts shown for FRouter role assignments (ADMIN_ROLE/EXECUTOR_ROLE) beyond initialize granting DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE only; role provisioning is external/missing in deployment context.

proof_of_signal.pattern: graduate() transfers full assetBalance from pair to msg.sender without checking tokenInfo state or one-time guard

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: privileged drain without state machine guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Liquidity extraction can occur even if token not meant to graduate yet
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check of graduation threshold, no 'already graduated' flag, no recipient restriction

analysis_questions: Who receives EXECUTOR_ROLE in production (Bonding, multisig, automation)?
Is graduate intended to only be callable from Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap (and is that enforced anywhere)?
Can an executor repeatedly call graduate to drain newly accumulated asset balances (fees)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment/role assignment transactions for FRouter
Simulate calling graduate pre-threshold and observe pool impact
Check whether FPair/router interactions allow repeated extraction

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If attacker obtains EXECUTOR_ROLE (misconfig/compromise), they call graduate(token) to pull all asset tokens from the pair to themselves, collapsing the market and stealing assets.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Drain of all assetToken liquidity from targeted pairs; market collapse
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All traders/holders of tokens in the bonding system
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low; requires admin intervention and recapitalization

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring graduate is only callable in correct state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track Transfer events from pair tokenB to executor

validation_result.mechanism: privileged drain without state machine guard
validation_result.impact_summary: Any EXECUTOR_ROLE holder on FRouter can drain the entire assetToken balance from any FPair to themselves at any time, without graduation conditions being met and without any one-time execution guard.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The FRouter.graduate() function at lines 168-174 transfers the full assetToken balance from a pair directly to msg.sender with no checks beyond the EXECUTOR_ROLE gate. There is no verification that (1) graduation conditions are met (e.g., reserveA <= gradThreshold), (2) the token is in a valid trading state, or (3) graduate has not already been called for this token. The function is designed to be called by Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap() during the graduation flow, but FRouter itself enforces none of these invariants. Since the same EXECUTOR_ROLE is required for buy(), sell(), addInitialLiquidity(), graduate(), and approval(), any entity granted EXECUTOR_ROLE for trading operations also gains the ability to drain pair assets via graduate(). The funds are sent to msg.sender rather than a fixed/configured recipient, meaning the caller receives the assets directly.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the code. FRouter.graduate() (lines 168-174) performs no state validation and sends assetToken to msg.sender. Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap() (which is the intended caller) performs its own state checks (trading && !tradingOnUniswap) but these are in Bonding, not in FRouter. FRouter has no way to verify the caller's intent or state. The EXECUTOR_ROLE is a coarse-grained permission that bundles critical financial operations (graduate/drain) with routine trading operations (buy/sell).

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Transfers full assetToken balance from pair to msg.sender with no graduation condition checks, no one-time guard, and no recipient restriction. Any EXECUTOR_ROLE holder can call this at any time for any pair.

The state machine checks (trading && !tradingOnUniswap) exist in Bonding, not in FRouter. FRouter.graduate() can be called independently of this flow by any EXECUTOR_ROLE holder, bypassing all state validation.

calling_sequence: 3. factory.getPair(tokenAddress, assetToken) returns the pair address
4. IFPair(pair).assetBalance() returns the full assetToken balance held by the pair
5. FPair(pair).transferAsset(msg.sender, assetBalance) sends all assetToken to the caller
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: FRouter.graduate() at line 168 requires only EXECUTOR_ROLE. At line 170, it gets the pair. At line 171, it reads the full asset balance. At line 172, it calls FPair.transferAsset() which at FPair.sol:92-95 uses IERC20(tokenB).safeTransfer(recipient, amount) to send all assets to msg.sender. No state checks exist in the entire path besides the role check. The function can be called for any token at any time, regardless of whether that token has met graduation criteria.

deployment_assumptions_checked: EXECUTOR_ROLE is assigned to at least one externally reachable contract or operational EOA beyond strictly trusted automation
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for FRouter show EXECUTOR_ROLE assignment. v2/upgradeFRouter.ts only handles the proxy upgrade. Bonding.sol must have EXECUTOR_ROLE since it calls router.buy(), router.sell(), router.graduate() etc. The exact set of EXECUTOR_ROLE holders is not visible in deployment scripts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: If an attacker obtains EXECUTOR_ROLE (through compromise of a hot wallet, misconfiguration, or being one of multiple EXECUTOR_ROLE holders), they call graduate(tokenAddress) for any active pair. The function transfers the entire assetToken balance to them with no checks. The pair's liquidity is drained, collapsing the bonding curve market. Even without compromise, Bonding contract having EXECUTOR_ROLE means a bug in Bonding could inadvertently trigger graduate() outside the intended flow. Furthermore, if EXECUTOR_ROLE is granted to operational contracts for buy/sell purposes, those contracts also inherit the ability to call graduate().
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH - The function enables complete drain of assetToken from any pair by any EXECUTOR_ROLE holder. This is a critical financial operation with no internal safeguards. The code defect is the absence of state machine guards (graduation threshold check, trading state check, one-time guard) and the use of msg.sender as recipient instead of a fixed address. Even if EXECUTOR_ROLE is tightly controlled, the function represents an unguarded drain primitive that violates defense-in-depth principles for critical financial operations.
recommended_mitigations: Add state validation inside graduate(): verify the token has met graduation criteria (e.g., check with Bonding contract or maintain state in FRouter)
Add a one-time execution guard: mapping(address => bool) public graduated; require(!graduated[tokenAddress]); graduated[tokenAddress] = true;
Transfer assets to a fixed, configured address (e.g., the Bonding contract) rather than msg.sender
Consider a separate role (e.g., GRADUATE_ROLE) distinct from EXECUTOR_ROLE to limit who can trigger graduation vs. normal trading
Add an event and consider a timelock for graduation operations
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FFactory, FRouter, FPair with test tokens. 2. Grant EXECUTOR_ROLE to both a Bonding contract and a test EOA. 3. Create a pair and add liquidity via Bonding.launch(). 4. From the test EOA (not Bonding), call FRouter.graduate(tokenAddress). 5. Verify the entire assetToken balance is transferred to the test EOA. 6. Verify this works even though the token hasn't met any graduation threshold.
uncertainties: Exact set of EXECUTOR_ROLE holders in production is not visible in deployment scripts
Whether operational procedures prevent misuse is unknown (off-chain controls)
Whether additional EXECUTOR_ROLE holders beyond Bonding exist in production
additional_testing_needed: Enumerate all EXECUTOR_ROLE holders on FRouter in production
Test calling graduate() from non-Bonding EXECUTOR_ROLE holder
Test calling graduate() before graduation threshold is met
Test calling graduate() multiple times (once for accumulated fees after initial graduation)
